title: Authors and Citation
text: Greifer N (2026). arg: Clean Simple Argument Checking. R package version 0.1.0, https://ngreifer.github.io/arg/.
code: @Manual{,   title = {arg: Clean and Simple Argument Checking},   author = {Noah Greifer},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0},   url = {https://ngreifer.github.io/arg/}, }

title: Clean and Simple Argument Checking
text: arg produces nice, clean error messages checking function arguments R packages. developed arg often found writing error messages packages, wasn’t satisfied similar R packages available. arg uses cli produce clean clear errors without requiring much programming developer. messages designed clear user, using complicated language. Many messages adapt type (incorrect) input received. arg contains function common arguments checks, checking argument single number (arg_number()), string (arg_string()), single TRUE/FALSE value (arg_flag()), data frame matrix (arg_data()). addition, possible build complex argument checks using arg_and() arg_or(), require least one check passed, when_not_null() when_supplied(), allow NULL omitted arguments. arg meant used inside R packages. error-throwing function err() (wrapper rlang::abort()) automatically includes relevant (user-facing) function error message. name “arg” meant short “argument”, also perhaps represents sound make encounter error running code. Hopefully arg reduces number times users say “arg!”.
code: library(arg)  z <- "a string"  # Check that `z` is a string arg_string(z)  # Check that `z` is a number arg_number(z) #> Error: #> ! `z` must be a number.  # Check that `z` is a string of length 2 arg_and(z,         arg_string,         arg_length(2L)) #> Error: #> ! All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✔ `z` must be a string #> ✖ `z` must have length 2  # Check that `z` is NA, a flag, or a count arg_or(z,        arg_is_NA,        arg_flag,        arg_count) #> Error: #> ! `z` must be NA, a logical value (TRUE or FALSE), or a count (a #>   non-negative whole number).

what: arg: Clean and Simple Argument Checking — arg-package
title: arg: Clean and Simple Argument Checking — arg-package
text: Checks function arguments, ideally use R packages. Uses simple interface produces clean, informative error messages using 'cli'.

title: arg: Clean and Simple Argument Checking — arg-package
text: Maintainer: Noah Greifer noah.greifer@gmail.com (ORCID)

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
text: Checks whether values within range (arg_between()), greater value (arg_gt()), greater equal value (arg_gte()), less value (arg_lt()), less equal value (arg_lte()).

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
code: arg_between(   x,   range = c(0, 1),   inclusive = TRUE,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL,   .call )  arg_gt(x, bound = 0, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_gte(x, bound = 0, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_lt(x, bound = 0, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_lte(x, bound = 0, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
text: x argument checked range vector two values identifying required range. inclusive logical vector identifying whether range boundaries inclusive . one value supplied, applied range boundaries. Default TRUE, throw error x equal boundaries. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. Passed err(). Set NULL omit call information. default search along call stack first user-facing function another package, . bound bound check . Default 0.

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
text: x checked type, possible values numeric values passed compared; however, error thrown typeof(x) equal typeof(range) typeof(bound). NA values x removed. arguments range, inclusive, bound checked appropriateness.

title: Check Values in Range — arg_between
code: z <- 2  try(arg_between(z, c(1, 3))) # No error try(arg_between(z, c(1, 2))) # No error try(arg_between(z, c(1, 2),                 inclusive = FALSE)) # Error #> Error : `z` must be between 1 and 2 (exclusive). try(arg_between(z, c(1, 2),                 inclusive = c(TRUE, FALSE))) # Error #> Error : `z` must be greater than or equal to 1 and less than 2.  try(arg_gt(z, 0))  # No error try(arg_gt(z, 2))  # Error #> Error : `z` must be greater than 2. try(arg_gte(z, 2)) # No error  try(arg_lt(z, 0))  # Error #> Error : `z` must be negative. try(arg_lt(z, 2))  # Error #> Error : `z` must be less than 2. try(arg_lte(z, 2)) # No error  try(arg_lte(z, "3")) # Error: wrong type #> Error : `z` must be less than or equal to "3".

title: Check Character Argument — arg_character

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
text: Checks whether argument atomic vector (arg_atomic()), dimensionless atomic vector (arg_vector()), list (arg_list()), data frame (arg_data.frame()), matrix (arg_matrix()), array (arg_array()), rectangular data set (arg_data()), environment (arg_env()).

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
code: arg_atomic(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_vector(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_list(x, df_ok = FALSE, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_data.frame(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_matrix(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_array(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_data(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_env(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. Passed err(). Set NULL omit call information. default search along call stack first user-facing function another package, . df_ok logical; whether allow data frames (technically lists). Default FALSE throw error data frame input.

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
text: Atomic vectors checked using .atomic(). matrices considered atomic vectors, arg_vector() additionally checks "dims" attribute, .e., length(dim(x)) == 0L. arg_data() checks whether x either data frame matrix. arg_list() checks whether x list; df = FALSE, throws error x data frame, even though data frames lists.

title: Check Common Argument Types — arg_data
code: vec <- 1:6 mat <- as.matrix(vec) dat <- as.data.frame(mat) lis <- as.list(vec) nul <- NULL  # arg_atomic try(arg_atomic(vec)) try(arg_atomic(mat)) try(arg_atomic(dat)) #> Error : `dat` must be an atomic vector. try(arg_atomic(lis)) #> Error : `lis` must be an atomic vector. try(arg_atomic(nul)) #> Error : `nul` must be an atomic vector.  # arg_vector try(arg_vector(vec)) try(arg_vector(mat)) #> Error : `mat` must be a vector.  # arg_matrix try(arg_matrix(vec)) #> Error : `vec` must be a matrix. try(arg_matrix(mat)) try(arg_matrix(dat)) #> Error : `dat` must be a matrix.  # arg_data.frame try(arg_data.frame(vec)) #> Error : `vec` must be a data frame. try(arg_data.frame(mat)) #> Error : `mat` must be a data frame. try(arg_data.frame(dat))  # arg_data try(arg_data(vec)) #> Error : `vec` must be a data frame or matrix. try(arg_data(mat)) try(arg_data(dat))

title: Check Supplied Dots Argument — arg_dots_supplied
text: arg_dots_supplied() checks whether arguments passed ... (.e., "dots") argument function. arguments evaluated. arg_dots_supplied() throws error ...length() 0. can useful function must additional arguments. example, arg_or() when_supplied() use arg_dots_supplied(). arg_dots_not_supplied() checks whether arguments passed ..., without evaluating arguments using ...length(). can useful function appears allow arguments (e.g., method generic), author want user supply .

title: Check Supplied Dots Argument — arg_dots_supplied
code: f <- function(...) {   arg_dots_supplied(...) }  try(f(1)) # No error: argument supplied try(f())  # Error! #> Error : An argument must be supplied to `...`.

title: Check Element — arg_element
text: Checks whether values argument elements set (arg_element()) elements set (arg_not_element()). arg_element() throws error (.element(x, values)) FALSE, arg_not_element() throws error (.element(x, values)) TRUE.

title: Check Element — arg_element
code: arg_element(x, values, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_not_element(x, values, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)

title: Check Element — arg_element
text: x argument checked values values check x . .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. Passed err(). Set NULL omit call information. default search along call stack first user-facing function another package, .

title: Check Element — arg_element
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Element — arg_element
text: arg_element() can useful checking whether argument matches one allowed values. important check done beforehand ensure x correct type, e.g., using arg_string() arg_character() values character vector. partial matching used; values must match exactly. Use match_arg() allow partial matching return full version supplied argument. arg_not_element() can useful ensuring duplication values argument set.

title: Check Element — arg_element
code: f <- function(z) {   arg_element(z, c("opt1", "opt2", "opt3")) }  try(f("opt1"))            # No error try(f(c("opt1", "opt2"))) # No error, all are elements try(f(c("opt1", "opt1"))) # No error: repeats allowed try(f("bad_arg"))         # Error: not an element of set #> Error : `z` must be one of "opt1", "opt2", or "opt3". try(f("opt"))             # Error: partial matching not allowed #> Error : `z` must be one of "opt1", "opt2", or "opt3". try(f(c("opt1", "bad_arg"))) # Error: one non-match #> Error : Each element of `z` must be one of "opt1", "opt2", or "opt3".  g <- function(z) {   arg_not_element(z, c("bad1", "bad2", "bad3")) }  try(g("bad1"))            # Error: z is an element #> Error : `z` must not be one of "bad1", "bad2", or "bad3". try(g(c("bad1", "opt2"))) # Error: at least one bad match #> Error : No element of `z` may be one of "bad1", "bad2", or "bad3". try(g("opt1"))            # No error: not an element try(g(c("opt1", "opt2"))) # No error, none are elements

title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal

Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal
text: Tests equality performed evaluating whether isTRUE(.equal(x, x2, ...)) TRUE FALSE.

title: Check Equal Arguments — arg_equal
code: f <- function(x, y, ...) {   arg_equal(x, y, ...) }  try(f(x = 1, y = 1))      ## No error try(f(x = 1L, y = 1.0))   ## No error despite type difference try(f(x = 1, y = 1.00001, ## No error, within tolerance       tolerance = .001))  try(f(x = 1, y = 2))       ## Error: different #> Error : `x` must be equal to `y`. try(f(x = 1, y = 1.00001)) ## Error #> Error : `x` must be equal to `y`.  g <- function(x, y, ...) {   arg_not_equal(x, y, ...) }  try(g(x = 1, y = 1)) ## Error #> Error : `x` must not be equal to `y`. try(g(x = 1, y = 2)) ## No error

title: Check Formula Argument — arg_formula
code: form1 <- ~a + b form2 <- y ~ a + b not_form <- 1:3  try(arg_formula(form1))    # No error try(arg_formula(form2))    # No error try(arg_formula(not_form)) # Error: not a formula #> Error : `not_form` must be a formula.  try(arg_formula(form1,                 one_sided = TRUE)) # No error try(arg_formula(form2,                 one_sided = TRUE)) # Error, not one-sided #> Error : `form2` must be a one-sided formula.  try(arg_formula(form1,                 one_sided = FALSE)) # Error, only one-sided #> Error : `form1` must be a two-sided formula. try(arg_formula(form2,                 one_sided = FALSE)) # No error

title: Check Index Argument — arg_index

title: Check Index Argument — arg_index
text: arg_indices(), error thrown unless one following true: x vector counts (see arg_counts()) less equal ncol(data) x character vector values subset colnames(data) arg_index(), x additionally must length equal 1. Passing arg_index() ensures data[, x] (data matrix) data[[x]] (x data frame) evaluate correctly. data column names, error thrown x character vector.

title: Check Index Argument — arg_index
code: dat <- data.frame(col1 = 1:5,                   col2 = 6:10)  f <- function(z) {   arg_index(z, dat) }  try(f(1))         # No error try(f(3))         # Error: not a valid index #> Error : `z` must be the name or index of a column in `dat`. try(f("col1"))    # No error try(f("bad_col")) # Error: not a valid index #> Error : `z` must be the name or index of a column in `dat`. try(f(1:2))       # Error: arg_index() requires scalar #> Error : `z` must be the name or index of a column in `dat`.  mat <- matrix(1:9, ncol = 3)  g <- function(z) {   arg_indices(z, mat) }  try(g(1))     # No error try(g(1:3))   # No error try(g("col")) # Error: `mat` has no names #> Error : `z` must be the index of a column in `mat`.

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
text: Checks whether argument specified class (arg_is()) specified class (arg_is_not()).

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
code: arg_is(x, class, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_is_not(x, class, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
text: x argument checked class character vector one classes check x . .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. Passed err(). Set NULL omit call information. default search along call stack first user-facing function another package, .

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
text: arg_is() arg_is_not() use inherits() test class membership. arg_is() throws error elements class(x) class. arg_is_not() throws error elements class(x) class. Sometimes can permissive; combining arg_is() arg_and() can useful requiring object multiple classes. Alternatively, combining arg_is_not() arg_or() can useful requiring object specific combination classes. See Examples.

title: Check Argument Class — arg_is
code: obj <- structure(list(1),                  class = "test")  try(arg_is(obj, "test"))            # No error try(arg_is(obj, c("test", "quiz"))) # No error try(arg_is(obj, "quiz"))            # Error #> Error : `obj` must inherit from class <quiz>.  try(arg_is_not(obj, "test"))            # Error #> Error : `obj` must not inherit from class <test>. try(arg_is_not(obj, c("test", "quiz"))) # Error #> Error : `obj` must not inherit from class <test/quiz>. try(arg_is_not(obj, "quiz"))            # No error  # Multiple classes obj2 <- structure(list(1),                   class = c("test", "essay"))  try(arg_is(obj2, c("test", "quiz")))     # No error try(arg_is_not(obj2, c("test", "quiz"))) # Error #> Error : `obj2` must not inherit from class <test/quiz>.  ## Require argument to be of multiple classes try(arg_and(obj2,             arg_is("test"),             arg_is("quiz"))) #> Error : `obj2` must inherit from class <quiz>.  ## Require argument to not be a specific combination of ## multiple classes try(arg_or(obj2,            arg_is_not("test"),            arg_is_not("essay"))) #> Error : `obj2` must not inherit from class <test> or must not inherit from class #> <essay>.

text: arg_named() works vectors, lists, data frames. check whether matrix column names, use arg_colnamed() (also works data frames, vectors lists).

title: Check Named Argument — arg_named
code: obj <- c(1,          B = 2,          C = 3)  try(arg_named(obj)) # Error: one name is blank #> Error : `obj` must have non-empty and non-NA names.  names(obj)[1L] <- "A" try(arg_named(obj)) # No error  obj2 <- unname(obj) try(arg_named(obj2)) # Error: no names #> Error : `obj2` must have non-empty and non-NA names.  # Matrix and data frame mat <- matrix(1:6, ncol = 2L) colnames(mat) <- c("A", "B")  try(arg_named(mat))    # Error: matrices are not named #> Error : `mat` must have non-empty and non-NA names. try(arg_colnamed(mat)) # No error  dat <- as.data.frame(mat) try(arg_named(dat))    # No error: data frames are named try(arg_colnamed(dat)) # No error  colnames(mat) <- NULL try(arg_colnamed(mat)) # Error: no colnames #> Error : `mat` must have non-empty and non-NA names.

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA
text: arg_no_NA() throws error anyNA(x) 0. arg_all_NA() throws error (.na(x)) FALSE. arg_is_NA() throws error length(x) 1 anyNA(x) FALSE. arg_no_NA() useful checking meaningful argument supplied. arg_all_NA() arg_is_NA() primarily used arg_or() denote NA allowed argument.

title: Check NA in Argument — arg_no_NA
code: f <- function(x) {   arg_no_NA(x)  ## x must not be NA }  try(f(1))           ## No error try(f(NA))          ## Error: x is NA #> Error : `x` must not be NA. try(f(c(1, NA, 3))) ## Error: x contains NA #> Error : `x` must not contain NA values.  f2 <- function(y) {   arg_all_NA(y) ## y must be NA }  try(f2(NA))          ## No error try(f2(c(NA, NA)))   ## No error try(f2(1))           ## Error: y is not NA #> Error : `y` must be NA. try(f2(c(1, NA, 3))) ## Error: y is not all NA #> Error : `y` must only contain NA values.

text: x argument checked .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. title: Check NULL Argument — arg_non_null

title: Check NULL Argument — arg_non_null
text: , NULL refers length-0 object, including NULL, logical(0L), list(), 3[FALSE], etc. arg_non_null() useful checking meaningful argument supplied. arg_null() primarily used arg_or() denote NULL allowed argument.

title: Check NULL Argument — arg_non_null
code: f <- function(x = NULL, y = NULL) {   arg_non_null(x) ## x must not be NULL   arg_null(y)     ## y must be NULL }  try(f(x = 1,    y = NULL)) ## No error try(f(x = NULL, y = NULL)) ## Error: x is NULL #> Error : `x` must be non-NULL. try(f(x = 1,    y = 1))    ## Error: y is non-NULL #> Error : `y` must be NULL.  # Any object of length 0 is considered NULL try(f(x = numeric())) ## Error: x is NULL #> Error : `x` must be non-NULL. try(f(x = list()))    ## Error: x is NULL #> Error : `x` must be non-NULL.  test <- c(1, 2)[c(FALSE, FALSE)] try(f(x = test))      ## Error: x is NULL #> Error : `x` must be non-NULL.  # arg_null() is best used in and_or(): f2 <- function(z) {   arg_or(z,          arg_null(),          arg_number()) }  try(f2(NULL)) ## No error; z can be NULL try(f2(1))    ## No error; z can be a number try(f2(TRUE)) ## Error: z must be NULL or a number #> Error : `z` must be NULL or a number.

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
text: Checks whether argument numeric, additional constraints desired. arg_numeric() simply checks whether argument numeric. arg_number() checks whether argument numeric scalar (.e., single number). arg_whole_numeric() arg_whole_number() check whether argument whole numeric vector scalar, respectively. arg_counts() arg_count() check whether argument non-negative whole numeric vector scalar, respectively.

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
code: arg_numeric(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_number(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_whole_numeric(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_whole_number(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_counts(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)  arg_count(x, .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x), .msg = NULL, .call)

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
text: whole number decided testing whether value integer (.e., using .integer()) abs(x - trunc(x)) < sqrt(.Machine$double.eps). tolerance used .equal() compare values.

title: Check Numeric Argument — arg_numeric
code: count <- 3 whole <- -4 num <- .943  try(arg_number(count))       # No error try(arg_whole_number(count)) # No error try(arg_count(count))        # No error  try(arg_number(whole))       # No error try(arg_whole_number(whole)) # No error try(arg_count(whole))        # Error: negatives not allowed #> Error : `whole` must be a count (a non-negative whole number).  try(arg_number(num))       # No error try(arg_whole_number(num)) # Error: not a whole number #> Error : `num` must be a whole number. try(arg_count(num))        # Error: not a count #> Error : `num` must be a count (a non-negative whole number).  nums <- c(0, .5, 1)  try(arg_number(nums))  # Error: not a single number #> Error : `nums` must be a single number. try(arg_numeric(nums)) # No error try(arg_counts(nums))  # Error: not counts #> Error : `nums` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values).

title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
text: x argument checked ... arg_*() functions unevaluated function calls applied x. See Examples. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. Passed err(). Set NULL omit call information. default search along call stack first user-facing function another package, .

title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
text: Returns NULL invisibly error thrown.

title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
text: arg_or(), error thrown x fails supplied checks. can useful argument can take one several input types. arg_and(), error thrown x fails supplied checks. less useful , checks can simply occur sequence without arg_and(), arg_and() can supplied ... argument arg_or() create complicated criteria. ... arguments can passed either functions, e.g.,   unevaluated function calls x argument absent, e.g.,   mixture . functions best provide clean error message composed error messages failed checks. many options, can yield complicated error message, use caution. arg_and() marks check (v) passed checks cross (x) failed checks.
code: arg_or(x,        arg_number,        arg_string,        arg_flag) arg_or(x,        arg_number(),        arg_string(),        arg_flag())

title: Check That Arguments Meet Some or All Criteria — arg_or
code: # `arg_or()` f <- function(z) {   arg_or(z,          arg_number,          arg_string,          arg_flag) }  try(f(1))      # No error try(f("test")) # No error try(f(TRUE))   # No error try(f(1:4))    # Error: neither a number, string, #> Error : `z` must be a single number, a string, or a logical value (TRUE or #> FALSE). #              #        or flag, but a vector  # `arg_and()` g <- function(z) {   arg_and(z,           arg_counts,           arg_length(len = 2),           arg_lt(bound = 5)) }  try(g(c(1, 2)))     # No error try(g(c(1, 7)))     # Error: not < 5 #> Error : All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✔ `z` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values) #> ✔ `z` must have length 2 #> ✖ each element of `z` must be less than 5 try(g(c(1.1, 2.1))) # Error: not counts #> Error : All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✖ `z` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values) #> ✔ `z` must have length 2 #> ✔ each element of `z` must be less than 5 try(g(4))           # Error: not length 2 #> Error : All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✔ `z` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values) #> ✖ `z` must have length 2 #> ✔ `z` must be less than 5 try(g("bad"))       # Error: no criteria satisfied #> Error : All of the following conditions must be met: #> ✖ `z` must be a vector of counts (non-negative whole numeric values) #> ✖ `z` must have length 2 #> ✖ `z` must be less than 5  # Chaining together `arg_and()` and `arg_or()` h <- function(z) {   arg_or(z,          arg_all_NA,          arg_and(arg_count,                  arg_lt(5)),          arg_and(arg_string,                  arg_element(c("a", "b", "c")))) }  try(h(NA))  # No error try(h(1))   # No error try(h("a")) # No error try(h(7))   # Error: not < 5 #> Error : At least one of the following conditions must be met: #> • `z` must be NA #> • `z` must be a count (a non-negative whole number) and less than 5 #> • `z` must be a string and one of "a", "b", or "c" try(h("d")) # Error: not in "a", "b", or "c" #> Error : At least one of the following conditions must be met: #> • `z` must be NA #> • `z` must be a count (a non-negative whole number) and less than 5 #> • `z` must be a string and one of "a", "b", or "c"

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
text: arg_symmetric() checks whether argument square, symmetric, numeric matrix. arg_cov() checks whether argument like covariance matrix (.e., square symmetric non-negative diagonal entries). arg_cor() checks whether argument like correlation matrix (.e., square symmetric values -1 1 ones diagonal). arg_distance() checks whether argument like distance matrix (.e., square symmetric non-negative entries zeros diagonal).

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
code: arg_symmetric(   x,   tol = 100 * .Machine$double.eps,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL,   .call )  arg_cov(   x,   tol = 100 * .Machine$double.eps,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL,   .call )  arg_cor(   x,   tol = 100 * .Machine$double.eps,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL,   .call )  arg_distance(   x,   tol = 100 * .Machine$double.eps,   ...,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .msg = NULL,   .call )

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric

Passed isSymmetric(). ... arguments passed isSymmetric() (eventually .equal()). .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .msg optional alternative message display error thrown instead default message. .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
text: functions check argument square, symmetric, numeric matrix. can useful function can accept covariance matrix, correlation matrix, distance matrix. arg_cov() throw error values diagonal less -tol. arg_cor() throw error values greater 1 + tol absolute value diagonal entries -1 - tol 1 + tol. arg_distance() thrown error values less -tol diagonal entries -tol tol. tolerance just slightly greater 0 allow numeric imprecision. checks semi-definiteness rank performed.

title: Check Symmetric Matrix Argument — arg_symmetric
code: set.seed(1234) mat <- matrix(rnorm(12), ncol = 3L) # Non square sym_mat <- -crossprod(mat)          # Square, symmetric cov_mat <- cov(mat)                 # Covariance cor_mat <- cor(mat)                 # Correlation dist_mat <- as.matrix(dist(mat))    # Distance  try(arg_symmetric(mat))     # Error: not square #> Error : `mat` must be a square, symmetric, numeric matrix. try(arg_symmetric(sym_mat)) # No error  try(arg_cov(sym_mat)) # Error: diagonal must be non-negative #> Error : `sym_mat` must be a square, symmetric matrix with non-negative values on #> the diagonal. try(arg_cov(cov_mat)) # No error  try(arg_cor(cov_mat)) # Error: values must be in [-1, 1] #> Error : All values in `cov_mat` must be between -1 and 1. try(arg_cor(cor_mat)) # No error  try(arg_distance(cor_mat))  # Error: diagonal must be 0 #> Error : `cor_mat` must be a square, symmetric matrix with zeros on the diagonal. try(arg_distance(dist_mat)) # No error

what: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
text: functions similar stop()/cli::cli_abort(), warning()/cli::cli_warn(), message()/cli::cli_inform(), throwing error, warning, message, respectively. Minor processing done capitalize first letter message, add period end (makes sense ), add information calling function.

title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
code: err(m, .call, .envir = rlang::caller_env())  wrn(m, immediate = TRUE, .envir = rlang::caller_env())  msg(m, .envir = rlang::caller_env())

title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
text: m message displayed, passed message argument rlang::abort(), rlang::warn(), rlang::inform(). .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. See call argument rlang::abort() details. Set NULL omit call information. default search along call stack first user-facing function another package, . .envir environment evaluate glue expressions . See rlang::abort() details. Typically need changed. immediate whether output warning immediately (TRUE, default) save warnings end execution (FALSE). See warning() details. Note default differs warning().

title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
text: err() throws error condition. wrn() throws warning condition invisibly returns formatted warning message string. msg() signals message invisibly returns NULL.

title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
text: functions simple wrappers corresponding functions rlang, namely rlang::abort() err(), rlang::warn() wrn(), rlang::inform() msg(), function almost identically cli versions. main differences additionally process input (capitalizing first character message adding period end needed, unless multiple strings provided). err() used inside arg_*() functions arg.

title: Throw an Error, Warning, or Message — err
code: f <- function(x) {   err("this is an error, and {.arg x} is {.type {x}}") }  try(f(1)) #> Error : This is an error, and `x` is a number.  g <- function(x) {   wrn("this warning displayed last", immediate = FALSE)   wrn("this warning displayed first") }  try(g(1)) #> Warning: This warning displayed last. #> Warning: This warning displayed first.  h <- function() {   msg("is a period added at the end?")   msg("not when the message ends in punctuation!")   msg(c("or when multiple",         "!" = "messages",         "v" = "are",         "*" = "displayed"))   msg("otherwise yes") }  h() #> Is a period added at the end? #> Not when the message ends in punctuation! #> or when multiple #> ! messages #> ✔ are #> • displayed #> Otherwise yes.

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
text: alternative match.arg() improved error messages via cli. Returns first choice x NULL, supports partial matching.

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
code: match_arg(   x,   choices,   several.ok = FALSE,   ignore.case = TRUE,   .context = NULL,   .arg = rlang::caller_arg(x),   .call )

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
text: x string (character vector several.ok = TRUE) match choices. NULL, first element choices returned. choices character vector valid values. several.ok logical; TRUE, x may contain one element. Defaults FALSE. ignore.case logical; FALSE, matching case sensitive, TRUE (default), case ignored. .context optional character string providing context error messages (e.g., function object name). Prepended error message supplied evaluated cli. .arg name argument supplied x appear error messages. default extract argument's name using rlang::caller_arg(). Ignored .msg supplied. .call execution environment currently running function, e.g. .call = rlang::current_env(). corresponding function call retrieved mentioned error messages source error. Passed err(). Set NULL omit call information. default search along call stack first user-facing function another package, .

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
text: character string (vector, several.ok = TRUE) matched element(s) choices. match, error thrown. several.ok = TRUE, error thrown least one match.

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
text: Partial matching supported via pmatch(). match found, error thrown listing valid choices. Checks run x prior matching: first, arg_supplied() used check whether x supplied; arg_string() (several.ok = TRUE) arg_character() (several.ok = FALSE) used check whether x valid string character vector, respectively. ignore.case = TRUE (default), initial case-sensitive match run, values x unmatched, second, case-insensitive match run. ignore.case = FALSE, first match run. ensures exact matches (content case) prioritized case-insensitive matches.

title: Argument Verification — match_arg
code: f <- function(z = NULL) {   match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"),             ignore.case = TRUE) }  try(f())            # "None" (first choice returned) #> [1] "None" try(f("partial"))   # "Partial" #> [1] "Partial" try(f("p"))         # "Partial" (partial match) #> [1] "Partial" try(f(c("e", "p"))) # Error: several.ok = FALSE #> Error : `z` must be a string.  # several.ok = TRUE g <- function(z = NULL) {   match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"),             several.ok = TRUE) }  try(g("exact"))               # Error: case not ignored #> [1] "Exact" try(g("Exact"))               # "Exact" #> [1] "Exact" try(g(c("Exact", "Partial"))) # "Exact", "Partial" #> [1] "Exact"   "Partial" try(g(c("Exact", "Wrong")))   # "Exact" #> [1] "Exact" try(g(c("Wrong1", "Wrong2"))) # Error: no match #> Error : `z` should be at least one of "None", "Exact", or "Partial".  h <- function(z = NULL) {   match_arg(z, c("None", "Exact", "Partial"),             .context = "in {.fun h},") }  try(h("Wrong")) # Error with context #> Error : In `h()`, `z` should be one of "None", "Exact", or "Partial".

title: Check Arguments When Supplied — when_supplied
text: error thrown x supplied fails one supplied checks (when_supplied()) NULL fails one supplied checks (when_not_null()). ... arguments can passed either functions, e.g.,   unevaluated function calls x argument absent, e.g.,   mixture . when_supplied() makes sense use argument default value can omitted. when_not_null() makes sense use argument default value NULL.
code: when_supplied(x,               arg_number,               arg_gt) when_supplied(x,               arg_number(),               arg_gt(bound = 0))

title: Check Arguments When Supplied — when_supplied
code: f <- function(z) {   when_supplied(z,                 arg_number,                 arg_between(c(0, 1))) }  try(f())    # No error: not supplied try(f("a")) # Error: not a number #> Error : When `z` is supplied, `z` must be a number. try(f(2))   # Error: not within 0-1 range #> Error : When `z` is supplied, `z` must be between 0 and 1 (inclusive). try(f(.7))  # No error: number within range  g <- function(z = NULL) {   when_not_null(z,                 arg_number,                 arg_between(c(0, 1))) }  try(g())     # No error: NULL okay try(g(NULL)) # No error: NULL okay try(g("a"))  # Error: not a number #> Error : When `z` is not NULL, `z` must be a number. try(g(2))    # Error: not within 0-1 range #> Error : When `z` is not NULL, `z` must be between 0 and 1 (inclusive). try(g(.7))   # No error: number within range
